title: Dog Bloody Diarrhea: Causes, Emergency Signs, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's bloody diarrhea an emergency? Learn the signs of HGE, why a photo helps your vet, and when to seek urgent veterinary care immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody diarrhea in dogs is a high-urgency symptom that requires immediate veterinary consultation to rule out life-threatening conditions like parvovirus or HGE. Seek emergency care if your dog also shows signs of lethargy, vomiting, or pale gums.

What does it mean if my dog has bloody diarrhea?

Bloody diarrhea, known medically as hematochezia (bright red blood) or melena (dark, tarry stool), indicates bleeding within the gastrointestinal tract. It is a distressing symptom that suggests the lining of the stomach or intestines has been compromised by inflammation, infection, or injury.

Is bloody diarrhea in dogs a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: High. You should treat any instance of bloody diarrhea as a potential emergency:

  • Bloody diarrhea can be caused by minor dietary indiscretion or life-threatening conditions such as Canine Parvovirus, Hemorrhagic Gastroenteritis (HGE), or toxin ingestion.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your dog exhibits lethargy, vomiting, or pale gums.

How does taking a photo of my dog's stool help the vet with triage?

  • A clear photograph helps determine the location of bleeding based on color (bright red vs. coffee-ground appearance).
  • It allows veterinarians to assess the ratio of blood to stool and the presence of mucus.
  • A photo enables the clinical team to determine severity and speed up the triage process before your dog even enters the exam room.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The acute onset of profuse hemorrhagic diarrhea, often described as resembling raspberry jam, is characteristic, particularly in small or toy breed dogs. Other common clinical signs include vomiting, anorexia, lethargy, and abdominal pain, with vomiting potentially preceding the diarrhea. Rapid fluid loss can lead to hypovolemic shock before noticeable dehydration occurs. In severe cases, tachypnea, tachycardia, marked depression, and explosive diarrhea leading to extreme dehydration may be present. Hypovolemic and endotoxic shock can manifest as poor capillary refill time, purplish mucous membranes, and cold extremities. Death can occur within 3 to 48 hours of the onset of clinical signs. Diagnostic findings often include hemoconcentration (PCV >55% or even >65%), and the complete blood count may reveal neutrophilic leukocytosis, although neutropenia raises concerns for sepsis or parvovirus. Fecal pathogen cultures (e.g., *Clostridium* spp, *Salmonella* spp, *Yersinia* spp, *Campylobacter* spp, enterotoxigenic *Escherichia coli*) and *Clostridium* spp enterotoxin evaluation by fecal ELISA can be considered.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 396)

faqs.question: Can I treat bloody diarrhea at home with a bland diet?
faqs.answer: No. Because blood indicates a breach in the intestinal barrier, there is a high risk of dehydration and sepsis. A bland diet is not sufficient until a veterinarian has ruled out serious underlying causes.

faqs.question: What if my dog is acting normally but has bloody stool?
faqs.answer: Even if your dog seems energetic, internal bleeding can lead to rapid decline. It is always safer to call your vet for a consultation to prevent a minor issue from becoming a crisis.

faqs.question: What information should I have ready for the vet?
faqs.answer: Be prepared to tell the vet when the diarrhea started, if they have eaten anything unusual, their vaccination history, and whether they are on any current medications.
